She expected the substantive caregiving work to require its own resources and capacity, which she sustained alongside the institutional fight. She did not expect, and did not seek, full official recognition of her scout or military service during her lifetime; the disposition was to wear down the bureaucratic refusal to whatever extent it would yield, while preserving the broader public record through other channels.

critical_incident_database.counterfactual: A competent peer — another Civil War-era figure with comparable claims for service compensation — would in many cases have either abandoned the fight after early refusals (the attrition failure mode) or converted it into a public denunciation that foreclosed the bureaucratic channels (the rupture failure mode). Few peers would have sustained a thirty-four-year institutional fight at the cadence of repeated petitions, affidavits, and re-filings, while preserving the substantive caregiving work and the public record on separable channels.
critical_incident_database.divergence_explanation: Harriet's divergence rests on the same long-arc structural-persistence disposition that selected the Auburn farm and the Garrett relationship and that sustained the rescue program across the 1850s. She treats institutional refusal as a process to be worn down at the operational tempo of the institution, not as a moral verdict to be denounced. The same disposition would later inform her caregiving work's institutional architecture (the AME Zion Church deed structure for the Home for the Aged) and her selective coalition engagement with white-led suffrage organizations (presence on terms she could control, without rupture-level confrontation).
critical_incident_database.outcome: The pension fight extended from 1865 to 1899. The 1899 grant of $20/month as 'nurse' was the partial recognition the bureaucratic terrain proved willing to provide. Full recognition of her scout or military service was never granted in her lifetime. The historical record, preserved through the Bradford biographies and through the public press, recorded the broader scope of her service. The pension funded her caregiving work at Auburn through her last years.

critical_incident_database.decision: In undertaking the 1869 Bradford biography Scenes in the Life of Harriet Tubman and the 1886 expansion Harriet, the Moses of Her People, Harriet maintained narrative agency through dictation, selection of which episodes to include, and curation of which witnesses (Garrett, Sanborn, Frederick Douglass) would supply confirmatory letters — refusing to surrender the construction of her own public record to either Bradford alone or to white abolitionist editors.
critical_incident_database.context: Harriet was illiterate and could not author a written record of her own life. By the late 1860s she was in financial distress (the pension fight had not yet succeeded), and she required a fundraising vehicle to pay debts and support her caregiving work. Sarah H. Bradford, an Auburn schoolteacher and writer, agreed to produce a biography. Harriet provided the substantive content through dictation; Bradford rendered it into prose. The 1869 first edition was followed by a substantially expanded 1886 edition, Harriet, the Moses of Her People, prepared after additional years of dictation and supplementary materials.

cdm_probes.cues_noticed: Harriet attended to the operational realities of mediated narrative construction: the writer (Bradford) was a literary mediator, not a substantive author; the witnesses' letters provided independent corroboration that the narrative alone could not establish; the selection of which episodes to include shaped the public understanding of her work; the choice of which witnesses to recruit shaped the credibility scaffolding around the narrative. She noticed that operational control of the narrative depended on her own active engagement at each of these dimensions, not on passive provision of dictated content.
cdm_probes.active_goals: Harriet's operative goals were: (1) produce a fundraising vehicle that would support her caregiving and pension work; (2) maintain narrative agency over the contents through dictation and selection; (3) curate the witness corroboration through deliberate choice of letters from Garrett, Sanborn, Douglass, and others; (4) preserve the operational compartmentalization of her work (she did not, even in dictation, disclose information that could compromise still-living persons whose identification might damage them); (5) construct the historical record on terms she could control rather than on terms a literary mediator alone would have selected.
cdm_probes.rejected_alternatives: Harriet rejected the alternative of leaving the biography's construction entirely to Bradford, which would have produced a writer-shaped narrative whose operational and historical priorities might have differed from her own. She rejected the alternative of declining to produce any biography, which would have foregone the fundraising and would have left the historical record to less-controlled sources (newspaper accounts, second-hand reminiscences). She rejected the alternative of including operationally compromising material (specific identifications, route details that might damage still-living persons), on the recognition that operational compartmentalization extended into the post-rescue documentation phase.
cdm_probes.situation_framing: Harriet framed the biography as a constructed instrument with operational, financial, and historical functions to perform — not as a memoir or a personal expression. Her own engagement at the dictation, selection, and witness-curation phases was the operational instrument through which the construction was directed. The frame is the same disposition that selected coded-religious-language for the operational letters (surface and substance separately designed), the rendezvous-in-woods architecture (positioning her body for operational utility), and the bounded acceptance of the pension's partial recognition (terrain-specific operational engagement).
cdm_probes.expected_outcome: Harriet expected the 1869 biography to produce sufficient fundraising for the immediate financial pressures, which it did. She expected the 1886 expansion to consolidate the historical record through additional dictation and witness corroboration, which it did. She expected to maintain narrative agency through her active engagement at the dictation, selection, and witness-curation phases, which she did. She expected the published biographies to be the principal vehicle for her public record during her lifetime, which they were.

critical_incident_database.counterfactual: A competent peer in the same situation — an illiterate operational figure requiring a literary mediator for biographical production — would in many cases have either provided dictation passively (yielding writer-shaped narrative control) or declined the biography (foregoing the fundraising and the historical record). Few peers would have actively curated witness corroboration, selected episodes for inclusion or exclusion on operational-historical grounds, and maintained narrative agency through the multi-phase construction process.
critical_incident_database.divergence_explanation: Harriet's divergence rests on the disposition to maintain operational control across construction phases, even when the construction medium (literary prose) is one she cannot author directly. She treats mediation as a constraint to be worked around through active engagement, not as a default of writer-shaped narrative. The same disposition would later inform her selective engagement with the white-led suffrage organizations (presence without alignment-level commitment) and her construction of the AME Zion Church deed for the Home for the Aged (active institutional design, not passive donation).
critical_incident_database.outcome: The 1869 Scenes in the Life of Harriet Tubman and the 1886 Harriet, the Moses of Her People were produced as designed. They served the fundraising function (revenues supported Harriet's debts and caregiving work), the historical function (preserving the public record of her work with witness corroboration), and the operational function (maintaining narrative agency through her active engagement). They remained, until late twentieth-century scholarly biographies, the principal published sources on her life.

critical_incident_database.decision: In 1903, Harriet deeded her Auburn property and adjacent acreage to the African Methodist Episcopal Zion Church to establish the Harriet Tubman Home for the Aged — selecting the AME Zion Church as institutional steward over alternative arrangements (private trust, secular philanthropy, family inheritance) on the calculation that the AME Zion structure provided the operational continuity, community legitimacy, and institutional durability that no alternative could match.
critical_incident_database.context: By 1903, Harriet was in her early eighties, the pension award had finally been granted ($20/month from 1899), her husband Nelson Davis had died (1888), and she had been operating an informal home for elderly and indigent black persons in her own residence at Auburn for years. The informal arrangement was unsustainable on her own. She had multiple options for institutional formalization: private trust, secular philanthropic endowment, family inheritance with caregiving obligation, or denominational stewardship. She chose the AME Zion Church, deeding the property and the adjacent acreage with the requirement that the institution carry forward as a home for the aged.

cdm_probes.cues_noticed: Harriet attended to the structural features of each institutional alternative: (1) a private trust required trustees whose multi-generational succession could not be guaranteed; (2) secular philanthropy depended on donor priorities that would shift over time; (3) family inheritance would burden her descendants with an institutional obligation they had not chosen; (4) the AME Zion Church had a multi-generational denominational structure, an existing operational infrastructure for community-care institutions, and a community legitimacy in the black population the institution would serve. She noticed that the AME Zion option provided the operational continuity, legitimacy, and durability that the alternatives could not match.
cdm_probes.active_goals: Harriet's operative goals were: (1) ensure the operational continuity of the home for the aged beyond her own lifetime; (2) embed the institution within a community structure whose legitimacy would persist; (3) preserve the home's purpose (care for elderly and indigent black persons) through institutional design rather than through donor or trustee discretion; (4) accept the operational constraints of denominational stewardship (church-managed governance, denominational priorities) as the cost of the durability gain; (5) construct the deed instrument so that the institutional obligation was binding on the AME Zion Church regardless of subsequent denominational decisions.
cdm_probes.rejected_alternatives: Harriet rejected the alternative of private-trust stewardship, on the recognition that trustee succession could not be guaranteed across multiple generations. She rejected the alternative of secular philanthropic endowment, on the recognition that donor priorities would shift. She rejected the alternative of family inheritance with caregiving obligation, on the recognition that the obligation would either burden her descendants or be abandoned. She rejected the alternative of an unincumbered gift to the AME Zion Church without binding institutional purpose, on the recognition that the Church's discretion would not necessarily preserve the home's specific function.
cdm_probes.situation_framing: Harriet framed the institutional design as a multi-generational durability problem requiring a steward whose multi-generational continuity was structurally guaranteed. The AME Zion Church was selected because its denominational structure provided the structural guarantee. The frame is the same long-arc disposition that selected the Auburn farm in 1857 (long-term family seat), sustained the Garrett relationship across the rescue decade (depth of trust over distributed risk), and sustained the pension fight across thirty-four years (institutional terrain to be worn down at its own tempo).
cdm_probes.expected_outcome: Harriet expected the AME Zion Church to formally accept the deed and the institutional obligation, which it did. She expected the Home for the Aged to take some years to become operationally established, which it did (it formally opened in 1908, five years after the deed). She expected her own remaining years to be spent partly within the home she had established, which they were. She expected the institutional structure to outlast her own lifetime by an indefinite multi-generational period.

critical_incident_database.counterfactual: A competent peer — another aging operational figure with similar caregiving obligations — would in many cases have selected private-trust stewardship (preserving secular control), secular philanthropic endowment (maximizing donor latitude), or family inheritance (preserving family connection). Few peers would have explicitly selected denominational stewardship for its multi-generational continuity, accepting the operational constraints of denominational governance as the cost of the durability gain.
critical_incident_database.divergence_explanation: Harriet's divergence rests on the long-arc structural-durability disposition visible across her career: trade present operational latitude for multi-generational structural continuity. The same disposition selected the Auburn farm, the Garrett relationship, the pension fight, and the operational compartmentalization at the Still handoff. She optimizes for institutional outlasting rather than for personal control.
critical_incident_database.outcome: The Harriet Tubman Home for the Aged was deeded to the AME Zion Church in 1903 and formally opened in 1908. Harriet herself entered the home in her last years and died there on March 10, 1913. The institution continued to operate as a home for the elderly through the twentieth century and remains, as of the early twenty-first century, an active historical site under AME Zion Church stewardship — the multi-generational continuity Harriet's 1903 institutional design was constructed to achieve.

critical_incident_database.decision: In July 1896, Harriet attended and spoke at the founding convention of the National Association of Colored Women in Washington, D.C. — selecting an institutional alignment with the black-women-led organization rather than with the white-led suffrage organizations (NAWSA factions) whose post-Fifteenth-Amendment ruptures had not been resolved on terms compatible with black women's standing.
critical_incident_database.context: By 1896 the white-led suffrage movement had been operating since the 1869 American Equal Rights Association rupture in two principal factions, neither of which had fully integrated black women's standing into its operational priorities. The National Association of Colored Women (NACW) was founded at the July 1896 convention in Washington, D.C., explicitly as an organization of and for black women. Tubman, then in her seventies and a celebrated figure, attended the convention, was honored, and delivered remarks that were preserved in the convention proceedings.

cdm_probes.cues_noticed: Harriet attended to the structural difference between the white-led suffrage organizations and the black-women-led NACW: the white-led organizations had not resolved the post-Fifteenth-Amendment rupture on terms that recognized black women's specific standing; the NACW was operationally constituted to address that standing without preliminary negotiation. She noticed that her own participation at events (rather than at organization-level alignment) was the operational instrument she could use without compromising her base in the AME Zion Church and the broader black community.
cdm_probes.active_goals: Harriet's operative goals were: (1) lend her name and presence to the founding of an institution that addressed the operational standing of black women; (2) preserve her own operational base in the AME Zion Church and the broader black community by aligning with the NACW rather than with the white-led suffrage factions; (3) support the suffrage cause without subordinating that support to white-led organizational priorities; (4) accept the bounded form of her engagement (attendance and remarks, not organizational office) as appropriate to her stage of life and her existing operational commitments.
cdm_probes.rejected_alternatives: Harriet rejected the alternative of more extensive engagement with the white-led suffrage organizations (which she had attended occasionally over the prior years), on the recognition that the post-1869 rupture had not been resolved on compatible terms. She rejected the alternative of declining the NACW founding event, on the recognition that her presence and name lent operational weight to the new institution. She rejected the alternative of organizational office at the NACW, on the recognition that her operational base remained the AME Zion Church and the caregiving work at Auburn.
cdm_probes.situation_framing: Harriet framed the question of suffrage-organization engagement as a selective coalition entry problem in which the institution-level alignment had to be selected for compatibility with her existing operational base. The white-led organizations failed the compatibility test on the standing question; the NACW met it. The frame is the same disposition that selected St. Catharines as the Canadian operational base (proximate to her work, compatible with her network), the Auburn property (proximate to family and political access), and the AME Zion Church for the Home for the Aged (denominational continuity with the community served).
cdm_probes.expected_outcome: Harriet expected her presence at the NACW founding to be honored and to lend operational weight to the new institution, which it did. She expected her bounded engagement (attendance and remarks, not office) to preserve her existing operational commitments at Auburn and within the AME Zion Church, which it did. She expected the NACW to provide an institutional structure for black women's organizational work that the white-led organizations had not provided, which it did over its subsequent decades.

critical_incident_database.counterfactual: A competent peer — another senior figure with credentials in both abolition and suffrage — would in many cases have aligned with the more visible white-led suffrage organizations on the calculation that their broader political reach made them the operationally dominant institutions. Few peers would have explicitly selected the new black-women-led organization on the basis of compatibility with the existing operational base, accepting the smaller institutional scale as appropriate to the alignment requirements.
critical_incident_database.divergence_explanation: Harriet's divergence rests on the disposition to select coalition alignments for compatibility with the existing operational base, rather than for the apparent reach or visibility of the coalition. The same disposition is visible in her selection of the Garrett station (depth over distribution), the AME Zion Church for the Home for the Aged (community continuity over secular reach), and the bounded engagement with John Brown (strategic alignment without tactical adoption). Each coalition is selected on the basis of compatibility, not visibility.
critical_incident_database.outcome: Harriet attended the July 1896 NACW founding convention and was honored as an elder figure of the black-women-led organizational tradition. The NACW continued through the twentieth century as a principal organization of black women's institutional work. Her own engagement remained bounded — presence at events, remarks, name on the institutional record — without office or operational diversion from the Auburn caregiving work.

critical_incident_database.decision: Across her operational career — including at least the Christmas 1854 rescue, the 1857 parents' rescue, multiple late-1850s missions, and the Combahee planning — Harriet integrated the visions and sleep seizures arising from her c.1834 head injury into operational decision-making, using them to time departures, choose routes, and maintain morale, as a heuristic supplement to (not a replacement for) her standard operational planning.
critical_incident_database.context: From the c.1834 head injury onward, Harriet experienced sleep seizures with vivid imagery she interpreted within her Methodist providential framework as direct communications from God. These were continuous over her life. Her operational use of them is documented across multiple rescues: she would defer a departure if a vision warned her, change a planned route if a vision indicated a different path, and declare divine guarantee of safe arrival to maintain morale among her parties. Contemporaries — Frederick Douglass, William Still, Sarah Bradford, Wendell Phillips — repeatedly attested to the operational effectiveness of these decisions, regardless of theological interpretation.

cdm_probes.cues_noticed: Harriet attended to the visions as operational signals whose source she interpreted theologically but whose content she evaluated operationally. She noticed that the visions sometimes coincided with subsequent operational developments she could not have consciously predicted (a patrol on a planned route; a betrayal at a planned station; a window of safety at an unscheduled time). She noticed that the visions' operational integration produced a confidence and party-morale effect that explicit probabilistic reasoning could not have produced. She noticed that the visions did not replace her standard planning but supplemented it as a heuristic input.
cdm_probes.active_goals: Harriet's operative goals were: (1) integrate the visions as a heuristic input alongside her standard operational planning; (2) act on the visions when they diverged from the standard plan, without requiring articulable reasons that would have been theologically and operationally unnecessary; (3) maintain party morale through the providential framing, which provided a confident leader whose decisions were not visibly contestable; (4) accept the theological and operational separability of the visions' source (which she interpreted providentially) and content (which she evaluated operationally); (5) preserve the visions as operational instruments whose effectiveness was empirical rather than theoretical.
cdm_probes.rejected_alternatives: Harriet rejected the alternative of treating the visions as either purely religious experience (without operational integration) or as purely seizure phenomenon (without operational meaning). She rejected the alternative of suppressing the visions or attempting to medicalize them, on the recognition that their operational utility outweighed any cost of integration. She rejected the alternative of explicit articulation of the visions' content to her parties in detail, on the recognition that the providential framing was operationally sufficient and that detailed articulation would have invited skeptical contestation.
cdm_probes.situation_framing: Harriet framed the visions as operational signals whose source-interpretation (providential) and content-evaluation (operational) were separable variables to be optimized independently. The frame is the same disposition visible across her decision-making: separable variables, independently selectable, deployed for their operational function. The visions were one such variable, integrated into the broader decision architecture without requiring either theological theorization or medical reduction.
cdm_probes.expected_outcome: Harriet expected the visions to continue throughout her life as a heuristic supplement; expected their operational integration to produce, on average, better decisions than purely conscious planning would have; expected the providential framing to maintain party morale; expected contemporaries to attest to the operational effectiveness regardless of their own theological interpretations. The historical record bears out each expectation: contemporary testimony repeatedly attests to the operational effectiveness; her record across thirteen missions records zero passengers lost.

critical_incident_database.counterfactual: A competent peer — another conductor with similar operational responsibilities and similar religious framework — would in many cases have either suppressed the unusual cognitive experiences (treating them as distractions) or treated them as purely religious experience without operational integration (preserving theological clarity at the cost of operational integration). Few peers would have integrated unusual cognitive-perceptual experiences as operational heuristic supplements while maintaining the standard operational planning architecture intact.
critical_incident_database.divergence_explanation: Harriet's divergence rests on the same separable-variables-independently-deployed disposition visible across her operational career. She does not collapse source and content; she does not collapse theological interpretation and operational evaluation; she does not collapse standard planning and heuristic supplement. Each is held as a separable input to be deployed for its operational function. The same disposition selected the coded-religious-language letters (surface and substance separately designed), the directional misdirection of the Tilly route (apparent direction and operational direction separately selectable), and the bounded engagement with the white-led suffrage organizations (presence and alignment separately calibrated).
critical_incident_database.outcome: The visions continued throughout Harriet's life. Her operational integration of them across at least thirteen documented rescue missions produced a record of zero passengers lost and zero conductor capture under her direction — a record that contemporaries and historians have variously attributed to her standard operational planning, her providential intuition, statistical luck, or some combination. The operational record is robust to the theological or medical interpretation of the visions' source. The integration of the unusual cognitive instrument with the standard operational architecture is the divergent disposition.
